item.content:encoded: I really like SCPs that are big objects in space and this is one of those. The idea of a sort of living asteroid prison that disguises itself with anti-memes is really cool. The end with the prisoner being freed and flying away was a nice change of pace instead of some ultra evil humanity destroying god suddenly popping out - I hope that 5959-B wasn't lying and is now happy to just chill out in space.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There’s also the potential that it doesn’t care about the Foundation, and is merely going for those who imprisoned it first as an act of vengeance before going about doing what it was doing before it’s imprisonment. Whether it was exploring the universe, destroying things, or worse, we have no clue whatsoever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In the GOI Ranking Survey, there was a comment made about CotSH that I have similar sentiments about:

They have a lot of potential I feel but I have yet to see THE article on them that makes me fall in love with them.

For me, this is that article, or at least close enough to it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
NI——CE——
A good SCP
I am the Chinese translator of the SCP.
I must say it is actually a wonderful story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ohhhh, my bad. I’ll get that fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey, whilst making a translation of your SCP, i found another small numeric oddity: 5959 is supposed to measure about 110 x 80 m (which lacks a dimension, btw), yet it easily can manifest 70 m broad crevasses or hills with damaging falls and has enough gravity to keep Orpheus in orbit?
Could it be you've meant kilometers? The number's don't add up elseway.

EDIT: I see you tried to fix it, but turning it into km² wasn't what i meant. (You just described a 4 dimensional surface).
A 3D object has length, height and width, three measurments. 110 x 80 is lacking the third.
Sorry for bothering you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It had a difficult time holding my attention at first, (probably a problem on my end, though), until I read these comments and pieced all the seemingly-arbitrary pieces together.

I intrinsically find the idea of two people telling conflicting super interesting, and, given the aforementioned problem on my end, find myself thinking through both possible scenarios a lot, trying my damnedest to piece together which one is lying.

Thank you for making me mentally exercise lmao, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn, I think you’re right. Will fix.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not sure if my math is right, but at 0.6c shouldn't -B exit the heliosphere in a bit over a day?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You see, that's the thing. We have all this build-up, the entire asteroid pleading with the Foundation to turn back. Whatever's in there is clearly bad news. And then when this thing escapes, all it wants to do is see the stars?

One of them is lying.

Either -B was imprisoned wrongly, forgotten and trapped alone for who knows how long in a maddening, chaotic hellscape when all it wanted was to see the stars, or it's going to majorly fuck things up for the universe and just doesn't want the Foundation to know. And we have no idea which is true.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well that was pretty incredible! The ending has me a little weirded out, though; the entire Freya log is essentially a bunch of gods screaming and pleading with the Foundation to turn back, and you're expecting it to be a story about the Foundation poking an antimemetic bear with a stick to see what happens. But then the Foundation doesn't suffer at all at the end because this thing just wanted to… see the stars?

I really like the escalation in the Freya logs and I really like the idea of something escaping a cosmic prison to live amongst the stars, but those feel like two different narratives.

But I can ignore that slight objection. An easy +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: First of all: I really appreciate that you enjoyed this so much! I was a little iffy about mixing those two topics, and I'm glad I managed to execute it well. Second of all, you have a point there- I think I'll tweak the end a bit to reflect that the skip isn't really gone. Thanks for the tip!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Uh, i really like this one. Church of the Second Hytoth meets Antimemes. In Space!

There is only on thing that bothers me: why would they think about calling this Neutralized?
We know of atleast one still-anomalous thing being out there (-B), and the main anomaly still appears to be somewhat functional (throwing off error messages and being antimemetic).

Anyway, this is great work, you deserve more upvotes.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
“There is an appallingly large percentage of the world which no human has ever properly looked at. This is unbearably limiting to all forms of antimemetic research. Antimemetic biology, antimemetic paleontology, antimemetic cosmology, antimemetic archaeology… These disciplines, all of them, barely exist. They are nowhere.”
-Immemorial by qntm

Suffice it to say, this got me thinking. Thanks so much to Westrin and Researcher Sparrow for their kind critting of the draft for this article on Discord!

+ Spoilers
- Hide

So, the Foundation accidentally released an elder god. Or did they?

A few things to consider:

  • Either 5959 or 5959-B is lying. The Foundation has no idea which.
  • We don’t know the truth either, although an educated guess can be made.

Image here. Do not sue the sword.
